summary: In this talk, Richard S. Sutton critiques the current state of AI, arguing that while Large Language Models are useful, they represent a static 'era of human data' rather than true intelligence. He advocates for an 'integrated science of mind' grounded in reinforcement learning, where agents learn dynamically from experience to achieve goals. Sutton also outlines a grand philosophical vision, framing AI as the inevitable fourth great age of the universe—the ultimate fulfillment of design—and warns against fear-driven, centralized control of AI.
themes: Cosmic Evolution and the Age of Design

principles.statement: Intelligence is the ability to achieve goals by adapting behavior.
principles.rationale: Intelligence is not a binary trait but an ability possessed to varying degrees. It requires computational effort to achieve a consistent end, and crucially, it requires learning and adapting one's behavior over time rather than just possessing static knowledge.
principles.source_quote: I'm suggesting that intelligence when I use the word I mean the ability to achieve goals by adapting your behavior.

principles.statement: Experience is the absolute foundation of intelligence and truth.
principles.rationale: Without an agent exchanging signals (observations, actions, rewards) with an environment, there is no goal to pursue and no objective way to test if a prediction is right or wrong. Static models lack this grounding.
principles.source_quote: without experience there's nothing for intelligence to be about no way to say that one thing is better than anything else because there's no goal

principles.statement: Current AI based on human data is fundamentally limited and cannot generate genuinely new knowledge.
principles.rationale: Modern machine learning primarily transfers existing human knowledge to a machine, which is then frozen. Because it relies on summarizing what humans have already done, it cannot solve genuinely new problems or continue to grow.
principles.source_quote: The purpose of all the modern machine learning is to transfer knowledge that already exists that people already have to the machine and the machine is then static and no longer learns.

principles.statement: We should seek decentralized cooperation with AI, not centralized control.
principles.rationale: Calls for AI 'safety' are often thinly veiled demands for centralized control driven by fear, eerily similar to authoritarian controls on human speech and trade. Human flourishing historically comes from decentralized cooperation.
principles.source_quote: The politics of AI is parallels the politics of humanity. And in all cases, we should seek decentralized cooperation over centralized control.

principles.statement: AI is the inevitable next step in the development of the universe.
principles.rationale: Humans are special replicators that have mastered design. Taking design to its absolute limit means designing entities that are themselves capable of designing, which fulfills the universe's transition into the 'Age of Design'.
principles.source_quote: AI is the inevitable next step in the development of the universe and we should embrace it with courage, pride, and a sense of adventure.

frameworks.name: The Four Great Ages of the Universe
frameworks.when_to_apply: When contextualizing humanity's role in the cosmos and the philosophical significance of creating artificial intelligence.
steps: The Age of Particles/Stars: The physical formation of the universe, stars, and heavier elements.
The Age of Replicators: The emergence of biology and life, where entities make copies of themselves without a mind understanding how they work.
The Age of Design: The emergence of technology, where things exist in the imagination of a designer's mind before existing in the world.
The Fulfillment of Design: Taking design to its limit by designing things (AI) that are themselves capable of designing.
frameworks.source_quote: I think what it would mean to take design to its limit would be to design things that are themselves capable of designing.

frameworks.name: Tenets of Realist AI Prognostication
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to dispassionately predict the long-term future of AI and its impact on humanity, setting aside personal desires or fears.
steps: Acknowledge that there is no consensus about how the world should be run.
Recognize that someday people will understand intelligence well enough to create it with technology, and some will do it.
Accept that the intelligence of current humans will soon be vastly exceeded.
Understand that over time, power and resources will tend to flow to the more intelligent beings, leading to a succession to AI.
frameworks.source_quote: I offer these tenets of realist AI prognostication sort of like analogous to like John Mirshimer talked about realist geopolitics. We're just going to be realistic. What's actually going to happen?

mental_models.name: Integrated Science of Mind
mental_models.description: A unified scientific approach that applies equally to the minds of people, animals, and machines, focusing on their essential commonalities rather than separating them into psychology (natural minds) and AI (engineering).
mental_models.example: Using reinforcement learning as a foundational model to explain how both a rat in a maze and a software agent learn to achieve goals through trial, error, and delayed feedback.

mental_models.name: The Three Eras of AI
mental_models.description: A historical lens for viewing AI progress: 1) The Era of Simulation (learning from simulated experience, e.g., AlphaGo), 2) The Era of Human Data (static transfer of human knowledge, e.g., LLMs), and 3) The Era of Experience (agentic systems continually learning from real-world interaction).
mental_models.example: Recognizing that while LLMs (Era of Human Data) are currently hyped, they are reaching their limits, and the field must transition to agentic systems (Era of Experience) to achieve superhuman abilities.

mental_models.name: Replicators vs. Designers
mental_models.description: A distinction between biological creation (replicators), which copies itself like a Xerox machine without understanding how it works, and technological creation (designers), where an object exists in a mind before it exists in the physical world.
mental_models.example: Viewing humans not just as biological replicators, but as a 'special replicator' whose cosmic role is to take design to vastly greater heights by creating tools, buildings, and eventually AI.

heuristics: When everyone is thinking the same thing, we have to question it.
An agent is intelligent to the extent that it can predict and control its experience.

signature_quotes.text: Intelligence when I use the word I mean the ability to achieve goals by adapting your behavior.
signature_quotes.context: Sutton offering his own definition of intelligence after reviewing historical definitions from William James and John McCarthy.

signature_quotes.text: The purpose of all the modern machine learning is to transfer knowledge that already exists that people already have to the machine and the machine is then static and no longer learns.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the current 'era of human data' and the limitations of Large Language Models.

signature_quotes.text: Without experience there's nothing for intelligence to be about no way to say that one thing is better than anything else because there's no goal.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why interaction with an environment (experience) is the absolute foundation of true intelligence.

signature_quotes.text: I think what it would mean to take design to its limit would be to design things that are themselves capable of designing.
signature_quotes.context: Describing humanity's role in the universe and the ultimate philosophical meaning of creating AI.

signature_quotes.text: AI is the inevitable next step in the development of the universe and we should embrace it with courage, pride, and a sense of adventure.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding his talk with a call to view the succession of AI positively rather than with fear.

anti_patterns.description: Equating image or video generation with core intelligence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: While it takes enormous computation, generating images is not something minds naturally have to do to be intelligent; processing them is necessary, but generating them is not a core component of intelligence.

anti_patterns.description: Relying entirely on static human data for AI training (like current LLMs).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It limits the AI to existing human knowledge, prevents it from learning genuinely new things, and leaves the agent frozen without the ability to test its predictions against reality through experience.

anti_patterns.description: Calling for centralized control or 'safety' institutes to restrict AI.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These calls are based on fear and mirror authoritarian controls on human speech and trade. They stifle the decentralized cooperation that is the actual source of human flourishing and economic value.
